_meta.notes: Ch3 L6. Filipino dishes: adobo, sinigang, kare-kare.

cards.body: Every Filipino family has a version of these dishes. They're the heart of Filipino cooking.
Adobo is the unofficial national dish. Meat braised in vinegar, soy sauce, garlic, and bay leaves.

example.note: Every family's adobo recipe is different. And every family thinks theirs is the best.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'Adobo' comes from the Spanish 'adobar' (to marinate). But the cooking technique of preserving meat in vinegar existed in the Philippines before the Spanish arrived.

cards.body: Sinigang is a sour soup. Usually with pork or shrimp, tamarind broth, and vegetables.

example.note: The sour broth is the star. Perfect for rainy days.

cards.source_text: Lola is cooking meat in vinegar, soy sauce, and garlic.
cards.explanation: Adobo = meat braised in vinegar, soy sauce, garlic, bay leaves.

cards.body: Kare-kare is a rich peanut stew. Oxtail, tripe, or seafood with vegetables in thick peanut sauce.

example.note: Often served at special occasions and fiestas.

cards.explanation: Adobo = braised, Sinigang = sour soup, Kare-kare = peanut stew.
Nagluto si Nanay ng sinigang. nag- (past) + si (name marker) + ng (object marker).

vocabulary.meaning: meat braised in vinegar and soy sauce

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: Wrong answers go to back of current session queue.

alam_mo_ba: There's a heated national debate over whether adobo should have an official recipe. The government tried in 2021. Filipinos were not having it. Every family's version is sacred.
Sinigang was voted the best soup in the world by TasteAtlas in 2021.
Kare-kare's peanut sauce is often paired with bagoong (fermented shrimp paste). The sweet-salty combination is addictive.
